title: Chapter Twenty-Seven: Reunion
content:
## JEONG YEON-SHIN

They climbed Zhongnan Mountain together.

Jung-san walked close, stealing glances at the blue robe, at the Desolate Sword, at the way Yeon-shin's posture had changed. The brother he had last seen as a boy in the ashes of the Jeong family manor was now something else entirely.

"Hye is being taught by the Zhongnan Sword Immortal," Jung-san said.

"What?"

"She hasn't officially become a disciple. But the teachings are almost equivalent."

The Zhongnan Sword Immortal. A swordsman said to surpass even Elder Yeo Il-sin. He rarely descended to the secular world, but his reputation stretched across the Central Plains.

*Hye has been recognized.*

Something warm moved behind the door in his chest. He let it come, just a crack.

"More than that," Jung-san said, his expression complicated. "You... have become incredibly strong. A formal martial artist of Desolate Sect."

"Strong?" Yeon-shin shook his head. "I'm still far from it."

He was sparing with his words. Even to his brother. Only the Desolate Sect Lord knew about the shortened lifespan, and he had no desire to widen that circle.

"Master Jeong's ambition is brilliant," Wei Ji Myo-hwa said from ahead. "You must have already received a blue robe over the white uniform."

*It's not ambition. It's desperation.*

He said nothing. Jung-san's expression shifted with each of the Sword Dragon's words -- a Zhongnan disciple watching his younger brother treated like an honoured guest by the greatest martial artist in the sect.

They passed the mountain gate. Antique pavilions spread before them, modest compared to Desolate City but threaded with the unmistakable weight of tradition. Wood weathered by centuries. Stone paths worn smooth by generations of swordsmen's feet.

"The sect leader and the great elder have gone to battle the Tyrant Sword Sect Leader," Wei Ji Myo-hwa said. "If the guests are from Desolate Sect, we should meet the highest elder remaining."

They climbed several rugged peaks. Crossed a training ground edged with mist.

And there, walking out from behind a row of practice dummies, an old man and a young girl.

The girl's eyes went wide.

"Uncle!"

She ran. Not a martial artist's controlled sprint -- the full headlong rush of a child who had lost her parents overnight and found something she feared she had lost forever.

He caught her.

"Hye. Have you been well?"

"Uncle..."

She was sobbing against his chest. Small fists clenched in his robe. Won-chang, watching from the side, sniffled loudly and wiped his nose on his sleeve.

The door opened.

Not all the way. But the light coming through was warm, and he let it fall on his face for a moment before he put the child down and straightened.

The old man was watching him. A strange, measuring gaze. Sharp despite his age, and behind the sharpness, something vast.

"He's from Desolate Sect," Wei Ji Myo-hwa said to the old man. Her tone was the tone one used with a grandfather.

Yeon-shin and Won-chang clasped fists and introduced themselves.

The old man placed his hands behind his back.

"I am Jong Yeo-il."

"The Zhongnan Sword Immortal!"

Won-chang's shout echoed off the cliff walls. He slapped both hands over his mouth. No one blamed him.

"I pay my respects to the Sword Immortal," Yeon-shin said calmly.

He had seen the Desolate Sect Lord from a distance. He had felt reverence for reputation before. This was different. This man had the feel of weathered stone -- something that had endured so long it no longer needed to prove its hardness.

*Even with such a master, they can't defeat the Tyrant Sword Sect in one blow?*

They exchanged simple words. He told the Sword Immortal their official purpose -- to observe the impact of the conflict on civilians -- and their private one: to confirm the safety of his blood relatives.

Jong Yeo-il stared at him. The measuring gaze deepened.

"How old are you?"

"Sixteen this year."

"Hmm." The old man raised his hand and pointed. "Would you like to see something?"

Yeon-shin followed the gesture. Through the mist at the peak's edge, a cliff face rose opposite them, its surface scarred with lines.

Not scratches.

Sword traces.

*Sword traces.*

He was already moving toward the edge. The trajectories carved into the rock face were sharp even through the weathering of decades -- long, sweeping arcs that ran the full length of the cliff, impossible unless the swordsman who made them could walk on empty air.

Won-chang stationed himself behind Yeon-shin, hands ready to grab the back of his robe if he leaned too far.

"The Sword Master, who passed away fifty-nine years ago, carved these," Jong Yeo-il said, appearing at his shoulder.

"Sword Master Ou Yang Chuan!" Won-chang blurted.

Yeon-shin did not look away from the cliff. "You know him?"

"The disciple he expelled established the Tyrant Sword Sect! All their sword techniques originate from the Sword Master!"

"He was the one who competed with your sect leader," Jong Yeo-il said, "and yet climbed Zhongnan Mountain holding my young hand. If not for the Desolate Sect Lord, there was almost no one in the world who could rival him."

The Sword Immortal spoke of his regret. An old man standing at the end of life, watching a young stranger stare at the only remaining trace of his benefactor.

"Many of our swordsmen tried to comprehend these traces," Jong Yeo-il said. "It was not a sword path that could be taken merely through broad knowledge or high martial arts. The masters who had some understanding of swords agreed: only a flash of inspiration could trace the Sword Master's path."

There was a phrase written in one stroke within the mist-shrouded rock.

*Who is worthy.*

"The sect's expectations are gathered on Myo-hwa," the Sword Immortal said. "I know even if I don't show it. It must be burdensome."

Wei Ji Myo-hwa's expression was unreadable. She, the unprecedented genius of Zhongnan Sect, did not deny the burden.

"What did you see?" Jong Yeo-il asked.

Yeon-shin silently drew the Desolate Sword.

---
